hints.Verus: 'Verus' is an adjective meaning 'true' — it modifies 'amicus'
hints.amicus: 'amicus' is a noun meaning 'friend' — it is the subject
hints.alter: 'alter' is a pronoun/adjective meaning 'another' — it is part of the predicate

hints.to eat: 'to eat' is 'edere' — 'est' here comes from 'esse' (to be)

hints.Imperfect: Imperfect of 'esse' would be 'erat' — 'est' is the present 3rd person singular
hints.Perfect: Perfect of 'esse' would be 'fuit' — 'est' is the present 3rd person singular
hints.Future: Future of 'esse' would be 'erit' — 'est' is the present 3rd person singular

hints.edo, edere, edi, esum: These are the principal parts of 'edere' (to eat)
hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the principal parts of 'stare' (to stand)
hints.fio, fieri, factus sum, —: These are the principal parts of 'fieri' (to become)

hints.Genitive: Genitive of 'amicus' would be 'amici' — 'amicus' is the nominative form
hints.Accusative: Accusative of 'amicus' would be 'amicum' — 'amicus' is the nominative form
hints.Vocative: Vocative of 'amicus' would be 'amice' — 'amicus' is nominative here as the subject

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nouns have genitive in -ae — 'amici' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension nouns have genitive in -is — 'amici' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -ūs — 'amici' ends in -i

hints.est: 'est' is a verb — adjectives modify nouns, not verbs
hints.alter: 'alter' is in the predicate — 'Verus' agrees with the subject 'amicus'
hints.ipse: 'ipse' is an intensive pronoun — 'Verus' agrees with nominative masculine 'amicus'

hints.another: 'another' is 'alter' — 'ipse' is an intensive pronoun meaning 'himself'

hints.other (of many): 'other (of many)' is 'alius' — 'alter' specifically means 'the other of two' or 'a second'
hints.changed: 'changed' would be 'alteratus' — 'alter' is a pronoun/adjective meaning 'another'

translation: A true friend is another self
